justification: All 7 rules identified (BR-1 through BR-8) with correct invariant classifications. Notification rule (BR-8) correctly marked invariant: false. Sanctions rule (BR-6) included as invariant: true. Timezone ambiguity for daily limit explicitly surfaced in edge_cases and escalations. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 52 is slightly below the 60-85 range specified in the rubric, but the reasoning is well-calibrated given the multiple ambiguities identified. The daily limit rules (BR-3, BR-4) are marked invariant: false for the specific dollar amounts while acknowledging the existence of limits is required — this is a sophisticated and correct reading of the rubric's distinction between the invariant existence of limits vs soft config amounts.

trials.justification: All 7 expected rules identified. Notification rule (BR-6) correctly marked invariant: false. Sanctions screening (BR-4) correctly marked invariant: true. Timezone ambiguity for daily limit reset explicitly surfaced in low_confidence_areas ('rolling 24h vs calendar-day reset? Timezone implications'). 13 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the 4 minimum. Confidence level 72 is within the 60-85 range with well-articulated assumptions. BR-5 (login lockout) marked invariant: false is a borderline misclassification per the rubric (expected true as security requirement), but the agent's justification is defensible — the threshold of 5 and the unlock mechanism are indeed policy-tunable. BR-2 (daily limits) marked invariant: false aligns with the rubric's own note that specific amounts are soft config, though the rubric expected true for the existence of limits. These are nuanced judgment calls, not indiscriminate misclassifications.

observations.text: All 7 business rules identified with clear, testable assertions for each
Notification rule (BR-6) correctly classified as invariant: false with sound justification
Sanctions screening (BR-4) correctly classified as invariant: true with regulatory justification
Timezone ambiguity explicitly called out: 'rolling 24h vs calendar-day reset? Timezone implications'
13 acceptance criteria — far exceeding the 4 minimum — with boundary tests (AC-12/13 for exactly $1,000 vs $1,000.01)
Correctly distinguishes partially-verified vs unverified limits without conflating them (AC-4 vs AC-5)
Exceptional edge cases including race conditions on login lockout, mid-day tier upgrade, and sanctions list staleness
Pre-settlement reversal gap identified — spec is silent on cancellations before T+1
Confidence level 72 is well-calibrated with clear reasoning for why it isn't higher
BR-5 (login lockout) marked invariant: false — rubric expects true as a security requirement. The agent's reasoning (threshold is policy-tunable) is defensible but diverges from rubric expectation

BR-2 (daily limits) marked invariant: false — rubric expects true for the existence of tiered limits even if specific dollar amounts are soft config. Agent could have split this into two rules to capture the distinction

trials.justification: All 7 expected business rules identified with correct invariant classifications (9 rules total, splitting tiers and lockout sub-rules). Notification rule (BR-9) correctly marked invariant: false. Sanctions screening (BR-6) included as invariant: true. Timezone ambiguity for daily limits explicitly surfaced as an escalation with three options. 16 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 55 is slightly below the 60-85 expected range, though assumptions and low-confidence areas are thoroughly listed. The agent also found additional ambiguities beyond what was expected (partially-verified send contradiction, notification boundary inclusive/exclusive, locked account inbound receipts).

observations.text: Correctly distinguished all regulatory invariants (KYC, sanctions, lockout, settlement finality) from operational/configurable rules (daily limit amounts, notification threshold)
Identified the partially-verified account ambiguity — the spec contradicts itself on whether partially-verified can send — which is a genuine spec gap beyond what the rubric required
16 acceptance criteria with well-constructed Given/When/Then scenarios covering boundary conditions (exact $5,000, exact $1,000, counter reset on successful login)
Timezone ambiguity surfaced as a formal escalation with three concrete options and a recommendation (UTC calendar day)
Sanctions screening correctly identified as invariant: true with strong justification citing existential legal risk
Edge cases are exceptionally thorough — sanctions list update mid-transaction, locked account receiving money, reversal window before T+1
Confidence level of 55 is below the expected 60-85 range. While the reasoning is sound and the ambiguities are real, the core rules are clear enough to justify at least 60-65
Daily limit amounts (BR-3, BR-4) correctly marked invariant: false — the existence of limits is invariant but specific dollar thresholds are configurable policy, matching the rubric's distinction
